title: Practice Midterm Q3 — Induction for $T(n) = T(n/2) + T(n/3) + n = O(n)$ (5 pts)
statement: Consider $T(n) = T(n/2) + T(n/3) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$. Provide a formal proof by induction that $T(n) = O(n)$.

steps.title: Step 1 — State the concrete claim.
steps.body:
We want $T(n) = O(n)$, which by definition means $\exists\, n_0 \in \mathbb{N}$ and $C \in \mathbb{R}^+$ such that $T(n) \le Cn$ for all $n \ge n_0$.

We'll prove by strong induction that $T(n) \le Cn$ for all $n \ge 1$, and pick $C$ at the end.

tips.label: Important: I.H. must be concrete
tips.content: Never write 'I.H.: $T(k) = O(k)$'. That's an asymptotic statement, meaningless as an inductive hypothesis. Use a concrete inequality like $T(k) \le Ck$.

steps.title: Step 2 — Base case.
steps.body: Try $n_0 = 1$: $T(1) = 1$. Need $1 \le C \cdot 1 = C$. So $C \ge 1$ suffices for the base case.

steps.title: Step 3 — Inductive step.
steps.body:
**Inductive hypothesis.** For all $1 \le k < n$, $T(k) \le Ck$.

Apply the recurrence and the I.H.:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}\right) + T\!\left(\tfrac{n}{3}\right) + n \;\le\; C\cdot\tfrac{n}{2} + C\cdot\tfrac{n}{3} + n = n\left(\tfrac{C}{2} + \tfrac{C}{3} + 1\right) = n\left(\tfrac{5C}{6} + 1\right).$$

We want this $\le Cn$, i.e. $\tfrac{5C}{6} + 1 \le C$, i.e. $1 \le C - \tfrac{5C}{6} = \tfrac{C}{6}$, i.e. $C \ge 6$.

tips.label: Must be EXACT
tips.content: If your inductive step ends with $T(n) \le (C+1)n$ or $T(n) \le 2Cn$, that does NOT finish the proof. Those extra constants compound across recursion levels. You must arrive at $T(n) \le Cn$ exactly, with the same $C$.

tips.label: Why it works here
tips.content: Because $1/2 + 1/3 = 5/6 < 1$. The sum of the fractions is the $a+b$ value, and whenever $a+b < 1$ the recurrence is $\Theta(n)$ — with $C = 1/(1 - (a+b)) = 1/(1 - 5/6) = 6$ always working.

steps.title: Step 4 — Pick $C$.
steps.body:
Base case needs $C \ge 1$; inductive step needs $C \ge 6$. Pick $C = 6$. Then both hold, completing the induction.

Therefore $T(n) \le 6n$ for all $n \ge 1$, so $T(n) = O(n)$. $\square$

answerKey: By strong induction with claim $T(n) \le 6n$: base case $T(1)=1 \le 6$; step $T(n) \le 6n/2 + 6n/3 + n = 3n + 2n + n = 6n$. Hence $T(n) = O(n)$.
